summary:
During radical neck dissection, most involved tissues such as nerves, lymph nodes, and part of the parotid gland may be excised; however, preservation of the submandibular gland ensures continued salivary secretion without major functional impairment or risk for postoperative xerostomia. The primary reason this specific organ remains intact pertains directly to maintaining essential physiological functions related to oral health maintenance through adequate moisture levels within the mouth via secretions produced by these glands. To summarize succinctly:

The submandibular gland stays untouched while performing a radical neck dissection since removing it could lead to severe dryness issues impacting speech clarity and overall quality-of-life aspects linked closely with proper hydration needs inside one’s mouth region after surgical intervention procedures targeting regional cancers located near vital anatomical landmarks like cervical neural pathways innervating facial musculature among others requiring meticulous attention towards preserving their integrity throughout treatment plans involving extensive resective surgeries aimed at eradicating malignancies threatening local regions around head & neck areas where normal bodily processes must continue uninterrupted despite aggressive therapeutic measures employed against them when
